I|will tell you what i will say , and do to such papist : 1. i will prove their objection false . and 1. that at this day all papist in the world be but ( as bishop Br•mhall estimate ) about a four part of the christian world . 2. and that it be not till the day of our king John , and their innocent the 3 penny , that a general council decree the idolatry of transubstantiation . 3. that a great part of their own never consent to this , and that few of the people understand or believe it . 4. that even this canon be make against great number of godly man , call Albigenses and Waldenses , who oppose they in this idolatry . 4. therefore i will resolve , i will have no local communion with any church in the use of this , or any idolatry , but will worship God in private , if i can have no better ; but if i can , i will . and i separate not by this , from the most of the church , but from a tyrannical corrupt sect or schism : yea , as to they , i hold mental communion with they in Christianity , and in all that be good and sound , and renounce communion with they in all that i know to be evil . Obj. but what if a protestant church make any sin a condition of their communion , will you not separate ? Ans. 1. i have say so much of this in this book , against the resolver , and reasonable defender , as that i be ashamed that man objection make i guilty of so much repetition . 2. none such can make any sin the condition of my mental communion : for if they join good and bad , and bid i do so , God forbid i , and require i to own the good , and disow the bad . if they use the bad themselves , and put not i to subscribe , or own it , i will join with they notwithstanding , in that which be good , and in due time and place , disow the evil , e . g . i have oft hear well-meaning man preach falsehood against calvanist , and other against arminians ; some against presbytery , some against independency , some against infant-baptism ; and alas ! how ordinary do man drop their error , and put they into their prayer ; i will not for this separate from a church that profess to to take the scripture for their rule . let they answer for their own misdoing . 2. but if they bid i subscribe , or approve any one falsehood or sin , i will deny it : if they forbid i communion , i will continue it till they put i away by force . and then it be not i that separate from they , but it|be they , by unjust cast i out , that be schismatical ; I|will still have mental communion with they in faith and love , and not persuade any to separate from they as idolater , or make they worse than they be ; but if i can , i will go to another church ( though worse ) that will receive i without impose actual sin ; but not draw other from they , who be not cast out for refuse sin , as i have be : and though i will not justify many protestant writer , who say , that we separate not from the church of Rome , but they cast we out for not sin ; yet i doubt not but this must be our case with sound church , that will impose any sin upon we . but still , to prefer the best and ( all thing consider ) most profitable , before the more faulty or imperfect , without renounce communion with they , or persuade all other from it , as idolater , or unlawful , be that which i never call schism , nor write against .

§ . 16. direct . 7. no man shall pretend danger or scruple against his renew the vow of Christianity , direct . 7. or any one essential part of it ; viz. to take God the father , son and holy ghost for my god , and saviour , and sanctifier , my owner , governor and father ; renounce the devil , the world and the flesh . because there be a absolute necessity ( praecepti and medii ) of perform this , and he that do it not , shall certain be damn : and therefore no worse matter can stand up against it : he that deny it , give up himself despair to damnation . yet i have hear many say , i dare not promise to turn to God , and live a holy life , lest i break this promise , and be worse than before : but do thou not know , that it must be both make and keep , if thou will be save ? when thou shall certain dye unless thou both take it , and keep it , and digest it . § . 17. direct . 8. about particular sin and duty , deliberate resolution be the ordinary mean of direct . 8. govern our life ; and vow must not be use where these will do the work without they . for extraordinary mean must not be use , when ordinary will serve turn . nor must you needless draw a double guilt upon your self in case of sin . and in mutable or doubtful case , a resolution may be change , when a vow can : try therefore what deliberate resolution will do , with the help of other ordinary mean , before you go any further . § . 18. direct . 9 when ordinary resolution and other help will not serve the turn , to engage the will direct . 9 to the forbearance of a know sin , or the performance of a know duty , but temptation be so strong as to bear down all , than it be seasonable to bind our self by a solemn vow , so it be cautelous and deliberate do , and no great danger like to follow . in such a case of necessity , 1. you must deliberate on the benefit and need : 2. you must foresee all the assault that you be like to have to tempt you to perjury , that they come not unexpected : 3. you must join the use of all other mean for the keep of your vow . § . 19 direct . 10. make not a law and religion to your self by your voluntary vow which God direct . 10. never make you by his authority : nor bound your self for futurity to all that be a duty at present , where it be possible that the change of thing may change your duty . give we enough . vow be not to make we new duty or religion , but to further we in the obedience of that which our lord have impose on we . it be a self-condemning sin of foolish will-worshipper , to , ( though they come far short of what be their duty ) ; and yet will be cut out more work for themselves . § . 20. and it be not enough that what you vow be your duty at the present , but you must bind your self to it by vow no long than it shall remain your duty . it may be your duty at the present to live a single life : but if you will vow therefore that you will never marry , you may bind your self to that which may prove your sin : you know not what alteration may befall you in your body or estate , that may invite you to it . be you sure that no change shall make it necessary to you ? or will you presume ▪ to bind God himself by your vow , that he shall make no such alteration ? or if you be never so confident of your own unchangeableness , you know not what fond and violent affection another may be possess with , which may make a alteration in your duty . at the present it may be your duty to live retire , and avoid magistracy and public employment : but you may not therefore vow it for continuance : for you know not but God may make such alteration , as may make it so great and plain a duty , as without flat impiety or cruelty , you can refuse : perhaps at the present it may be your duty to give half your yearly revenue to charitable and pious use : but you must not therefore vow it for continuance ( without some special cause to warrant it ) : for perhaps CHAP. IV. the unity of the spirit in the welfare of the church . ii as the unity of the spirit be the personal welfare of every christian , so be it the common interest of the church , and of all christian society , kingdom , city , school and family : and that in all these respect . I. unity be the very life of the church ( and of all society as such ) . the word life be sometime take for the live principle or form , and so the soul be the life of a man , and the spirit as dwell and work in we , be the moral or holy-spiritual life of the soul , and of the church as mystical : and sometime life be take for the union of the say vital principle with the organical body , or matter due unite in it self : and so the union of soul and body be the life of a man ; and the union of the political head and body be the life of political society : and so the union of Christ and the church be the life of the church ; and the union of the member among themselves , be ( as the union of the part of the organical body ) the necessary Dispositio materiae , without which it can have union with the head ; or the effect of union with the vital principle , and so the union which be essential to the church . as that be no body who part be not unite among themselves , nor no live body which be not unite to the soul ( and in it self ) ; so that be no church or no society which be not unite in it self ; and no christian society or church which be not unite unto Christ . it be a gross oversight of they that look at nothing but the regeneration of the member , as essential to the church , and take unity to be but a separable accident . yea indeed regeneration it self consist in the unite of person by faith and love to God and the redeemer and to the body of the church : and if union be life , then division be no less than death : not every degree of division : for some breach among Christians be but wound : ( but to be divide or separate from Christ , or from the iversal church which be his body , be death it self : and even wound must have a timely cure , or else they threaten at least the perish of the wound part . ) ii unity be the health , ease and quiet of the church and all society , as well as of each person : and division be its smart and pain : and a divide disagree society be a wound or sick society ; in continual suffering and disease : but how easy , sweet , and pleasant be it , when brethren dwell together in unity ? when they be not of many mind , and will and way ; when they strive not against each other , and live not in wrangle and contention , when they have not their cross interest , will and party , and envy not or grudge not against each other : but every one take the common interest to be his own ; and smart in all his brother suffering and hurt : when they speak the same thing , and mind the same interest , and carry on the same end and work ? o foelix hominum genus si vestros animos amor quo coelum regitur , regat , say Boetius . many contrivance good man have have , for the recover of the peace and felicity of society : and they that despair of accomplish it , have please themselves with feign such society as they think most happy : whence we have Plato be commonwealth , moor be Utopia , campanella Civitas solis , etc but when all be do , he be the wise and happy politician , and the best friend and benefactor to society and to mankind , who be the skilful contriver , and best promoter of unite love . i know that this be ( like life in man ) a work that require more than art : but yet i will not say hoc non est artis , sed pietatis opus , as if art do nothing in it : it be god work blessing man endeavour . Even in the propagation of natural life , though Deus and sol vivificant , God be the quickener , and fountain of all life ; yet man be the generator ( even if it prove true that the soul be create ) : and God will not do it without the act of man : so God will not bless church , and kingdom and family , with vniting-love , without the subordinate endeavour of man : and the skill and honesty of the endeavourer great conduce to the success of the work : man that stand in a significant capacity ( as ruler and public teacher do ) may do much by holy art to promote vniting-love in all society ; by contrive a unite of interest , ( and not by cudgel they all into the same temple or synagogue as prisoner into a jail ) ; and by diligent clear teach they the excellency and necessity of unity and love , and mischief of divide selfishness : but of this more after in due place . all the device in the world for the felicity of society which tend not unto unity , and all way of unity which promote not love , be erroneous and mere frivolous : and all that be contrary to love be pernicious , whatever the contriver pretend or dream . Iii . Unity be the strength and preservation of society , and selfishness and division be their weakness , their dissolution and their ruin . as in natural , so in political body , the close and perfect union of part , make the firm and most durable composition . what be the strength of a army but their unity ? when they obey one general commander , and cleave inseparable together , and forsake not one another in fight , such a army will conquer far great multitude of incoherent separable man : when every soldier think how to shift for himself , and to save his own life whatever become of other , a few run away first , and show the rest the way , and they be quick all make conquer fugitive : when they that resolve [ we must all stand or fall together , and we will not live or escape alone ; it be more than army than my life that i will preserve ] these be seldom overcome by any policy or power . what be the conquest of a army , but the rout and scatter of they ?
